what: This vignette demonstrates a full snow analysis workflow using a Sentinel-2 L1C scene (Stacked to a single tif, resampled to 20m) from the Austrian Alps (June 2023).
title: Snow Detection and Fractional Snow Cover with snowsense
text: workflow includes: 1. Data Loading 2. Snow Detection using different indices 3. Despeckling 4. Fractional Snow Cover Estimation using two different regression approaches ## Snow Detection snowsense supports three spectral indices snow detection. apply three scene comparison. understand indices work, best consult README original papers.  #Plot second plot showcases although Otsus method can work, produce precise results
code: # Load Packages  library(snowsense)  ds <- rast("../inst/extdata/example_s2_20m.tif") #load sentinel2 data as SpatRaster with terra  print(paste("Bands available:", paste(names(ds), collapse = ", "))) #> [1] "Bands available: B01, B02, B03, B04, B05, B06, B07, B08, B8A, B09, B10, B11, B12"  plotRGB(ds, r="B04", g="B03", b="B02", stretch="lin", main="Sentinel-2 L1C RGB Scene (20m, Stacked)") #lin2 result_ndsi <- detect_snow(ds, index = "ndsi",                              bands = list(green = "B03", swir = "B11"),                              threshold = 0.4)  # Note that if no threshold is set, the function falls back to Otsu's method. An unsupervised # approach that selects an optimal threshold for separating bimodal grayscale data into two classes result_ndsi_otsu <- detect_snow(ds, index = "ndsi",                                 bands = list(green = "B03", swir = "B11"),                                 threshold = NULL) #> Warning: [hist] a sample of 70% of the cells was used #> <SpatRaster> resampled to 500364 cells. #> <SpatRaster> resampled to 500364 cells. #> <SpatRaster> resampled to 500364 cells. #> <SpatRaster> resampled to 500364 cells. library(terra) x <- rast("../inst/extdata/example_s2_20m.tif") x <- x / 10000 writeRaster(x, "../inst/extdata/example_s2_20m.tif", overwrite = TRUE)

what: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
title: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
text: R package snow detection fractional snow cover estimation satellite imagery

title: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
code: # install.packages("devtools") # required to install packages from github devtools::install_github("Lutzkk/snowsense")

title: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
text: Detects snow covered pixels RGB multispectral imagery using spectral indices. x – SpatRaster containing image index – "ndsi", "wsi" "rgb_brightness" bands – named list band indices (e.g. list(green=3, swir=11)) – required bands depend chosen index threshold – numeric threshold binary snow/-snow classification; NULL, Otsu’s method (Otsu, 1979) applied automatically. determines optimal threshold maximizing variance snow non-snow pixel classes. list : index raster binary snow mask threshold value ndsi: Normalized Difference Snow Index (NDSI) exploits high reflectance snow visible spectrum strong absorption shortwave infrared (SWIR). NDSI = (R_green - R_SWIR) / (R_green + R_SWIR) commonly used threshold 0.4. rgb_brightness: Identifies snow based high brightness across visible spectrum. Simple fast may confuse snow clouds bright surfaces. Brightness = (R_red + R_green + R_blue) / 3 wsi: Water-resistant Snow Index (WSI) combines brightness hue RGN (Red-Green-NIR) space, making robust false positives water wet surfaces. WSI = (V - H) / (V + H) V pixel brightness (max R, G, NIR) H hue angle RGN space (Donmez et al., 2021)
code: tif <- system.file("extdata", "example_s2_20m.tif", package = "snowsense") x <- terra::rast(tif)  result <- detect_snow(x, index = "ndsi", bands = list(green = 3, swir = 10))  result$index        # index raster result$binary_mask  # binary snow mask result$threshold    # applied threshold

title: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
text: Removes speckle noise binary snow mask using majority filtering /minimum patch size. x – SpatRaster, binary snow mask (1 = snow, 0 = snow) window – odd integer, neighborhood window size majority filtering; NULL, skipped threshold – integer, minimum number snow pixels window classify center pixel snow; NULL, strict majority used min_pixels – integer, minimum number connected pixels snow patch retained; NULL, skipped SpatRaster — despeckled binary snow mask.
code: tif <- system.file("extdata", "binary_snowmask.tif", package = "snowsense") x <- terra::rast(tif)  # Majority-based despeckling clean <- despeckle_snow(x, window = 3)  # Also remove small isolated patches clean <- despeckle_snow(x, window = 3, min_pixels = 10)

title: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
text: Aggregates binary snow mask coarser resolution, returning fractional snow cover per cell. x – SpatRaster, binary snow mask (1 = snow, 0 = snow) fact – integer, aggregation factor (number pixels per direction) SpatRaster — continuous raster snow cover fraction (0–1) coarser resolution.
code: tif <- system.file("extdata", "binary_snowmask.tif", package = "snowsense") x <- terra::rast(tif)  fsc <- aggregate_snow(x, fact = 10)

title: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
text: Estimates Fractional Snow Cover (FSC) directly multispectral image using NDSI-based regression. Unlike aggregate_snow, operates original resolution estimates sub-pixel snow cover. x – SpatRaster, multispectral image bands – named list "green" "swir" band indices method – "salomonson" (linear regression, Salomonson & Appel 2004) "sigmoid" (Gascoin et al. 2020) SpatRaster — FSC values clamped [0, 1]. salomonson: Linear regression fitted MODIS data. FSC = 1.45 * NDSI - 0.01 (Salomonson & Appel, 2004) sigmoid: Sigmoid regression, robust high low NDSI values. FSC = 0.5 * tanh(2.65 * NDSI - 1.42) + 0.5 (Gascoin et al., 2020)
code: tif <- system.file("extdata", "example_s2_20m.tif", package = "snowsense") x <- terra::rast(tif)  scf <- snow_cover_fraction(x, bands = list(green = 3, swir = 10), method = "salomonson")

title: Tools for Snow and Avalanche Data Analysis
text: Donmez, C., Berberoglu, S., Cicekli, S. Y., Cilek, ., & Arslan, . N. (2021). Mapping snow cover using Landsat data: Toward fine-resolution water-resistant snow index. Meteorology Atmospheric Physics, 133(2), 281–294. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00703-020-00749-y Gascoin, S., Barrou Dumont, Z., Deschamps-Berger, C., Marti, F., Salgues, G., López-Moreno, J. ., Revuelto, J., Michon, T., Schattan, P., & Hagolle, O. (2020). Estimating fractional snow cover open terrain Sentinel-2 using normalized difference snow index. Remote Sensing, 12(18), 2904. https://doi.org/10.3390/rs12182904 Otsu, N. (1979). threshold selection method gray-level histograms. IEEE Transactions Systems, Man, Cybernetics, 9(1), 62–66. https://doi.org/10.1109/TSMC.1979.4310076 Salomonson, V. V., & Appel, . (2004). Estimating fractional snow cover MODIS using normalized difference snow index. Remote Sensing Environment, 89(3), 351–360. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2003.10.016

title: Despeckles snow patches based on a binary snow mask (1 == snow, 0 == no snow) — despeckle_snow
text: x SpatRaster - Binary snow mask (1 == snow, 0 == snow) window Integer - Odd-Sized neighborhood window majority filtering threshold Integer NULL - Neighborhood threshold.  NULL, strict majority used. min_pixels Integer - Minimum numbers connected pixels required snow patch retained.

title: Despeckles snow patches based on a binary snow mask (1 == snow, 0 == no snow) — despeckle_snow
code: #Basic majority-based despeckling snow_clean <- despeckle_snow( x, window = 3 ) #> Error: object 'x' not found  #Remove all isolated snowpatches snow_clean <- despeckle_snow( x, window=3, min_pixels=10 ) #> Error: object 'x' not found

what: Detects snow-covered areas from optical imagery using different spectral indices and brightness based approaches. — detect_snow
title: Detects snow-covered areas from optical imagery using different spectral indices and brightness based approaches. — detect_snow
text: Detects snow-covered areas optical imagery using different spectral indices brightness based approaches.

title: Detects snow-covered areas from optical imagery using different spectral indices and brightness based approaches. — detect_snow
code: detect_snow(   x,   index = "ndsi",   bands = list(aerosol = 1, blue = 2, green = 3, red = 4, red_edge1 = 5, red_edge2 = 6,     red_edge3 = 7, nir = 8, narrow_nir = 9, swir1 = 10, swir2 = 11),   threshold = NULL )

title: Detects snow-covered areas from optical imagery using different spectral indices and brightness based approaches. — detect_snow
text: x SpatRaster - tif containing multiple bands multispectral image index Character - Index use (currently supports "ndsi", "wsi" "rgb_brightness") bands Named list band indices green, swir, red, blue bands threshold Numeric NULL Threshold value snow detection. NULL, Otsu's method used determine threshold.

title: Detects snow-covered areas from optical imagery using different spectral indices and brightness based approaches. — detect_snow
text: Returns list three elements: indexed Tif (SpatRaster), binary snow mask (SpatRaster), threshold value(numeric).

title: Detects snow-covered areas from optical imagery using different spectral indices and brightness based approaches. — detect_snow
text: NDSI (Normalized Difference Snow Index) NDSI commonly used spectral index snow detection. utilizes high reflectance snow visible domain (usually green) strong absorption shortwave infrared (SWIR) domain. commonly used threshold 0.4. RGB Brightness method identifies snow based high brightness visible spectrum. Brightness computed mean reflectance red, green, blue bands. simple fast, approach may confuse snow clouds bright surfaces. Threshold depends datatype range input data (Otsu performs badly ). WSI (Water-Resistant Snow Index) WSI computed HSV-transformed red, green nir bands combining pixel-wise brightness (value) spectral color (hue), enabling robust discrimination snow-covered areas based high reflectance low spectral variability relative water vegetation. doi: 10.1007/s00703-020-00749-y

title: Detects snow-covered areas from optical imagery using different spectral indices and brightness based approaches. — detect_snow
code: # NDSI based snow detection detect_snow(x, index="ndsi",             bands=list(green=2,swir=4),threshold=NULL) #> Error: object 'x' not found # # RGB Brightness based snow detection detect_snow(x, index="rgb_brightness",             bands=list(red=3,green=2,blue=1),threshold=NULL) #> Error: object 'x' not found # WSI based snow detection detect_snow(x, index="wsi",             bands=list(red=3,green=2,nir=5),threshold=NULL) #> Error: object 'x' not found

what: Example Sentinel-2 RGB+SWIR raster (20 m) — example_s2_20m
title: Example Sentinel-2 RGB+SWIR raster (20 m) — example_s2_20m
text: small Sentinel-2 GeoTIFF included snowsense package demonstrations testing. raster contains four bands: Band 1 Coastal Aerosol (Sentinel-2 band 1) Band 2 Blue (Sentinel-2 band 2) Band 3 Green (Sentinel-2 band 3) Band 4 Red (Sentinel-2 band 4) Band 5 Red Edge 1 (Sentinel-2 band 5) Band 6 Red Edge 2 (Sentinel-2 band 6) Band 7 Red Edge 3 (Sentinel-2 band 7) Band 8 NIR (Sentinel-2 band 8) Band 9 Narrow NIR (Sentinel-2 band 8A) Band 10 SWIR 1 (Sentinel-2 band 11) Band 11 SWIR 2 (Sentinel-2 band 12)
